The EPA's cancer risk assessment for uranium indicates that long-term exposure increases the risk of bone cancer and liver cancer. While the risk at the 30 µg/L level is considered acceptable under EPA methodology, any radioactive exposure carries some risk. People exposed to uranium levels significantly above the MCL face elevated cancer risks that grow with dose and duration.

Vulnerable Populations

Children are at greater risk from uranium exposure than adults because they drink more water per unit of body weight and their kidneys are still developing. Pregnant women may also face increased risk because uranium can cross the placental barrier and expose the developing fetus. People with pre-existing kidney disease should be especially cautious about uranium exposure, as their kidneys are already compromised.

Which States and Regions Have the Highest Uranium Levels?

Uranium is not evenly distributed across the United States. Certain geological regions are naturally uranium-rich and account for the vast majority of elevated uranium in drinking water.

High-Risk Regions

The U.S. Geological Survey has identified several regions with consistently elevated uranium in groundwater:

  • The Western United States, particularly Colorado, Utah, Arizona, New Mexico, and Wyoming, where uranium-rich granitic and sandstone bedrock is common. This region accounts for the largest concentration of elevated uranium cases.
  • The Upper Midwest, including parts of Minnesota, Wisconsin, Michigan, and Illinois, where glacial deposits have concentrations of uranium-bearing minerals.
  • The Great Plains, particularly in parts of Kansas, Nebraska, South Dakota, and Oklahoma, where certain aquifer formations naturally contain elevated uranium.
  • Texas and Oklahoma, where both natural uranium deposits and historical uranium mining have left residual contamination.
  • Parts of the Northeast, including New England states, where granite bedrock formations contain uranium minerals.

However, elevated uranium can occur anywhere there are private wells, even in states where public water systems do not commonly report uranium problems. A homeowner in any state should consider testing if they use a private well, own property on granite bedrock, or live in an area with known uranium deposits.

How to Test Your Drinking Water for Uranium

Testing is the only way to know whether your drinking water contains uranium. If you are on a public water system, your water utility is required to test regularly and report results to customers. You can request a copy of your water quality report, called a Consumer Confidence Report, from your local water utility or find it on their website.

Testing Private Wells

If you have a private well, you are responsible for testing. The EPA recommends that all private well owners test for uranium at least once, and more frequently if you live in a known high-uranium area or have previously detected uranium.

To test your private well water for uranium:

  1. Contact your state health department or environmental agency for a list of certified laboratories that perform uranium testing. Many state agencies maintain lists of approved labs.
  2. Contact a certified lab directly and request a uranium analysis. The lab will provide you with sample bottles and instructions for collecting a water sample.
  3. Follow the lab's sampling instructions carefully. Uranium testing requires proper sample handling to avoid contamination or loss of uranium from the sample.
  4. Submit the sample to the lab and wait for results. Results typically come back within one to two weeks.
  5. Compare your results to the EPA's MCL of 30 µg/L. If your uranium level is at or above 30 µg/L, you should take steps to reduce your exposure.

A comprehensive well water test should also include uranium's radioactive daughter products, particularly radium, which often co-occurs with uranium in groundwater. Ask your lab about this when you order testing.

Using Free Online Tools

If you are on a public water system, you can use free databases to find your water utility's test results. ClearWater's free ZIP code lookup tool, available at checkclearwater.com, allows you to search for your water system and see reported contaminants including uranium. This is a quick way to learn whether uranium has been detected in your local public water supply.

Removing Uranium from Drinking Water

Several treatment technologies effectively remove uranium from drinking water. The best choice depends on your uranium level, budget, and water chemistry.

Reverse Osmosis

Reverse osmosis (RO) is one of the most effective and widely used methods for removing uranium and other radioactive contaminants. RO systems force water through a semi-permeable membrane that blocks uranium ions, along with other minerals and contaminants. Under-sink RO systems are affordable and require regular filter replacement but are highly effective at removing uranium. Whole-house RO systems are also available but are more expensive and require more maintenance.

RO systems typically remove 90 to 99 percent of uranium, depending on the system and water chemistry. One limitation is that RO produces wastewater (the water that does not pass through the membrane), so it is not perfectly water-efficient.

Activated Alumina Filters

Activated alumina is a porous form of aluminum oxide that absorbs uranium and other contaminants from water. Activated alumina filters are particularly effective for uranium removal and are used by some public water systems as well as in point-of-use filters. These filters work best in water with neutral pH and moderate mineral content.

Activated alumina filters require regular replacement as they become saturated with uranium. The frequency of replacement depends on the uranium concentration in your water and the size of the filter.

Anion Exchange Resins

Ion exchange resins can also remove uranium by swapping uranium ions for other ions that are harmless. These systems are typically larger and more expensive than point-of-use filters but can treat larger volumes of water. They require periodic regeneration or replacement.

Distillation and Boiling

Boiling water does not remove uranium. In fact, boiling concentrates uranium by reducing the total volume of water. Distillation, which involves boiling water and collecting the condensed steam, does remove uranium and other contaminants effectively. However, distillation is slow and energy-intensive, making it impractical for large-volume water needs. It can be useful for creating a small supply of safe drinking and cooking water.

Whole-House vs. Point-of-Use Treatment

You do not need to treat all the water in your home. For uranium and other radioactive contaminants, the primary exposure route is ingestion through drinking and cooking. Showering or bathing with water containing uranium does not pose significant health risk because uranium is not absorbed through the skin, and the exposure is brief. Most people choose to treat only drinking and cooking water using under-sink filters or pitcher filters, which is more cost-effective and practical than whole-house treatment.

Whole-house treatment may be appropriate if you have very high uranium levels, have children in the home, or have the budget to do so.

Steps to Take Today

If you are concerned about uranium in your drinking water, here are concrete actions you can take immediately:

If You Are on a Public Water System

  1. Request your water utility's most recent Consumer Confidence Report or find it online.
  2. Look for uranium in the list of contaminants tested. If uranium is not listed, it was not detected or was found at levels below the reporting limit.
  3. If uranium is listed and above 30 µg/L, contact your water utility and ask what treatment they are using and when they expect the problem to be resolved.
  4. Alternatively, use ClearWater's free lookup tool at checkclearwater.com to search your ZIP code and see what contaminants have been reported in your area.

If You Have a Private Well

  1. Contact your state health department and ask whether uranium testing is recommended for your area based on local geology.
  2. Get a quote from a certified laboratory for uranium testing. The test typically costs between 20 and 50 dollars.
  3. Collect a sample according to the lab's instructions and submit it for analysis.
  4. When results arrive, compare them to 30 µg/L. If your level is above 30, or if you have children or pregnant women in your home, consider treatment.
  5. If you decide to treat, research the cost and maintenance of reverse osmosis or activated alumina filters for your water use.

General Precautions

  • Avoid boiling tap water as a method to remove uranium, as this concentrates uranium rather than removing it.
  • Be especially cautious if you have children, are pregnant, or have kidney disease. These groups should prioritize uranium testing and treatment if elevated levels are found.
  • If you rent, ask your landlord about water testing for uranium. Tenants have the right to know about health risks in their water supply.

Conclusion

Uranium in drinking water is a real health hazard that affects millions of Americans, particularly those on private wells or living in naturally uranium-rich regions. The good news is that uranium is highly treatable with readily available technologies like reverse osmosis and activated alumina filters. By testing your water and understanding your exposure, you can make informed decisions to protect your family's health. Start with testing if you are on a well, and if you are on a public system, request your water quality report and check for uranium. Taking these steps today puts you in control of your drinking water safety.

title: New Jersey Tap Water: Lead, PFAS, and the Fight for Cleaner Water
excerpt: New Jersey's tap water faces serious contamination challenges including lead and PFAS. Learn about the Newark crisis, state regulations, and how to protect your family.
content:
When Newark, New Jersey's tap water turned brown in 2017, it exposed a crisis that had been quietly spreading through the city's aging pipes for years. Lead contamination affected thousands of families, making Newark's water quality emergency comparable to, and in some cases worse than, the infamous Flint, Michigan crisis. But Newark's story is just one chapter in New Jersey's complex water quality history. The state faces multiple overlapping challenges: legacy lead service lines installed decades ago, emerging PFAS (forever chemicals) contamination in several regions, and the challenge of maintaining water systems built in an era when water safety standards were far less stringent. This comprehensive guide explains what happened in New Jersey, what's happening now, and what you can do to protect yourself and your family.

The Newark Lead Crisis: When America's Water Systems Failed a City

In 2017, Newark's Department of Water and Sewerage discovered that lead levels in the city's drinking water had exceeded safe limits. What made this discovery particularly alarming was not just that lead was present, but that it had likely been there for years without adequate testing or public notification. Newark's water system serves approximately 580,000 residents, making it New Jersey's largest city. When officials began widespread testing, they found that lead levels in some homes exceeded 40 parts per billion (ppb), significantly higher than the EPA's action level of 15 ppb.

The crisis was severe enough to warrant comparison to Flint. In fact, by some measures, Newark's contamination was worse. While Flint's lead problem stemmed from a single decision to switch water sources, Newark's problem revealed systemic failures in water treatment, testing protocols, and infrastructure maintenance. The situation persisted for years before aggressive action was taken. Between 2017 and 2021, Newark distributed bottled water to residents and undertook an aggressive lead service line replacement program, but the damage to public trust had already been done.

How Did Lead Get Into Newark's Water?

Lead enters drinking water primarily through service lines, pipes, and fittings that contain lead. Newark's water system includes thousands of lead service lines installed before the 1986 ban on lead pipes. When water becomes corrosive (meaning it lacks adequate alkalinity or mineral content), it can leach lead from pipes into the water supply. Newark's water treatment plant wasn't properly treating the water to minimize corrosion, which exacerbated the problem.

Additionally, many homes built before 1986 have solder and brass fixtures that contain lead. Even if the main service line is copper, these components can be a source of contamination. Lead doesn't dissolve easily in water, but when water sits in pipes overnight or when corrosion occurs, it can migrate into your tap water. The first water drawn from a tap in the morning, or after hours of sitting, is most likely to contain elevated lead levels.

The Timeline and What Changed

The Newark crisis unfolded in stages. Initial testing in 2017 revealed the problem. By 2018, Newark began distributing bottled water to residents and launched testing and replacement programs. The state of New Jersey, recognizing the severity, provided emergency funding and oversight. By 2021, Newark had replaced over 20,000 lead service lines and improved water treatment practices, including increasing alkalinity to reduce corrosion. However, thousands of lead service lines remain in Newark and throughout the state.

PFAS Contamination: The Emerging Chemical Crisis

While Newark was dealing with lead, another contamination crisis was developing elsewhere in New Jersey. PFAS, shorthand for per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, are synthetic chemicals used in manufacturing, firefighting foams, and numerous consumer products. They're called forever chemicals because they don't break down in the environment or in human bodies. Several areas of New Jersey have detected PFAS contamination in their water supplies, and the state has become a national leader in regulating these chemicals.

Where Is PFAS Most Prevalent in New Jersey?

PFAS contamination in New Jersey is concentrated in specific regions. The Pinelands area of South Jersey has shown elevated PFAS levels, likely from historical industrial activity and landfills. More significantly, areas near Lakehurst Naval Air Station in Ocean County have experienced severe PFAS contamination. The military base used aqueous film forming foams (AFFF) containing PFAS for firefighting training for decades, and this contamination has migrated into groundwater and drinking water supplies serving nearby communities.

Other hotspots include areas near airports, manufacturing facilities, and landfills. If you live in central or southern New Jersey, particularly near any of these sites, PFAS testing should be a priority. You can use ClearWater's free ZIP code lookup tool to check whether PFAS has been detected in your specific area and which specific PFAS compounds have been found.

What Are PFAS and Why Are They Dangerous?

PFAS are chemicals with a carbon-fluorine bond, one of the strongest bonds in chemistry. This strength is what makes them useful for water-resistant coatings and firefighting foams, but it also means they don't break down. When ingested, PFAS accumulate in the human body, particularly in the liver. Scientific studies have linked PFAS exposure to kidney disease, liver damage, high cholesterol, thyroid disease, decreased vaccine response, and increased cancer risk. Unlike many contaminants, there's no clear safe threshold for PFAS exposure, which is why regulators are taking increasingly cautious approaches.

The two most studied PFAS compounds are PFOA and PFOS. For decades, the EPA had set the combined drinking water standard for these two chemicals at 70 ppb. In 2023, the EPA announced dramatically stricter standards: 4 ppt (parts per trillion) for PFOA and 4 ppt for PFOS, and interim standards for other PFAS compounds. To put this in perspective, 4 ppt is equivalent to one drop in an Olympic swimming pool.

New Jersey's Response: The Strictest PFAS Regulations in America

Recognizing the severity of PFAS contamination and the EPA's slow pace in regulating these chemicals, New Jersey has implemented some of the most aggressive PFAS regulations in the country. In 2018, New Jersey set a drinking water standard of 13 ppt for PFOA and 13 ppt for PFOS, significantly stricter than the federal EPA standard at that time. In 2023, when the EPA tightened its standards, New Jersey aligned with the new federal requirements.

New Jersey's Legal Frameworks for Water Safety

New Jersey's drinking water is regulated by the New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection (NJDEP), which often exceeds federal EPA requirements. The state requires regular testing for lead, PFAS, and hundreds of other contaminants. Water utilities must provide annual Consumer Confidence Reports (also called water quality reports) to all customers, detailing contaminant levels and health risks.

Additionally, New Jersey has been aggressive in requiring contaminated sites to be cleaned up. The state's Licensed Site Remediation Professional (LSRP) program and the Industrial Site Recovery Act (ISRA) require thorough investigation and remediation of contaminated properties before they can be redeveloped, reducing future contamination sources.

Lead Service Lines: A Slow-Motion Crisis Across New Jersey

While Newark drew national attention for its lead crisis, the state of New Jersey as a whole faces a massive infrastructure challenge. Thousands of lead service lines connect public water mains to homes throughout the state. These lines were standard before 1986, and replacing them is expensive, disruptive, and time-consuming.

How Many Lead Service Lines Remain in New Jersey?

Estimates suggest that New Jersey has between 400,000 and 600,000 lead service lines still in use. The exact number is difficult to pin down because many utilities haven't fully documented their systems. Older cities and towns, particularly in Essex, Hudson, Bergen, and Union counties, have the highest concentrations of lead service lines. Some utilities estimate that 20 to 40 percent of homes in their service areas are still connected via lead service lines.

Replacement Timelines and Current Progress

The EPA and NJDEP have set timelines for lead service line replacement. The goal is to replace 15 percent of the nation's estimated 9 million lead service lines every year. In New Jersey, major utilities have committed to replacement programs, but progress varies significantly.

Newark's aggressive replacement program, which began in 2018, has replaced tens of thousands of lines. Jersey City, which also experienced lead contamination issues, has a replacement program in place but is behind schedule. Smaller utilities across the state are moving more slowly, often limited by budget constraints and the complexity of coordinating replacements with residents and municipalities.

Most utilities are prioritizing areas with the highest lead contamination levels, schools, childcare facilities, and neighborhoods with older housing stock. However, at current replacement rates, it would take decades to replace all lead service lines in New Jersey. Renters and homeowners shouldn't wait for utilities to replace their lines. Testing your water is the first step toward understanding your risk.

Utility Comparisons: How Does Your Water System Perform?

New Jersey has multiple major water utilities serving different regions, and their performance on water quality varies. The largest utilities include Newark City Utilities, Jersey City Water Department, South Jersey Industries Water Company, and dozens of smaller municipal and regional systems.

Newark City Utilities Authority

Following the 2017 lead crisis, Newark has been under intense scrutiny and has improved its practices significantly. The utility has invested heavily in corrosion control, lead service line replacement, and testing infrastructure. However, trust remains damaged, and the system still requires significant ongoing investment to fully replace aging infrastructure.

Jersey City Water Department

Jersey City's water system also faced lead contamination issues that came to light around the same time as Newark's crisis. The city has implemented a lead testing program and is gradually replacing lead service lines. Like Newark, Jersey City's water comes from the Passaic River, which requires significant treatment to be safe for consumption.

South Jersey Industries Water Company

South Jersey Industries serves portions of Atlantic and Cape May counties. This utility's water primarily comes from groundwater sources, which have experienced PFAS contamination in some areas. The utility has invested in advanced treatment technologies to address PFAS contamination and has achieved compliance with state and federal standards.

Accessing Your Water Quality Report

Every water utility in New Jersey is required to publish annual Consumer Confidence Reports. These reports provide detailed information about contaminant testing, regulatory compliance, and potential health effects. You can request these reports from your local water utility, and they're often available on utility websites. If you're unsure which utility serves your home, using ClearWater's ZIP code lookup will tell you which system is responsible for your water and direct you to their quality reports.

Jersey City: A Case Study in Urban Water Infrastructure Challenges

Jersey City's experience with water quality issues provides important lessons for American cities nationwide. Like Newark, Jersey City relies on the Passaic River as a primary water source. The Passaic River is heavily industrialized and carries high levels of natural and anthropogenic contaminants. Water from this source requires extensive treatment, and even with modern treatment, occasional contamination breakthroughs occur.

The Corrosion Control Challenge

Jersey City, like Newark, discovered that its corrosion control treatment wasn't adequate. Lead was leaching from pipes and fixtures into the water supply. The city responded by adjusting treatment to increase pH and alkalinity, which creates a protective layer on pipe interiors and reduces lead leaching. However, this ongoing adjustment process requires continuous monitoring and expertise. Any lapse in treatment quality can quickly lead to elevated lead levels, which is why regular testing is essential.

What Jersey City Teaches Other Cities

Jersey City's challenges highlight that water quality crises aren't limited to poor or underfunded cities. Jersey City is a relatively affluent area with significant commercial and residential development. Its problems stem from aging infrastructure, complex water sources, and the technical challenges of treating surface water from industrial regions. These are problems that cities nationwide will increasingly face as infrastructure ages and climate change affects water availability.

What New Jersey Residents Can Do Today

Understanding the challenges facing New Jersey's water systems is important, but taking action to protect yourself and your family is more important. Here are concrete steps you can take today.

Test Your Water

The first step is knowing what's in your water. If you haven't had your water tested, do it now. You can purchase a home testing kit from hardware stores or send a sample to a certified laboratory. For lead specifically, flush your tap for 30 seconds before collecting the sample, then collect a sample that's been sitting overnight. This gives you both the best and worst-case scenarios. If you rent, contact your landlord and ask about water testing. Landlords are legally required to address lead issues in New Jersey.

Install Appropriate Filtration

While filtration isn't a replacement for fixing contamination at the source, it can reduce your personal exposure. For lead, use a certified pitcher or faucet-mounted filter that's NSF-certified to remove lead. For PFAS, standard filters don't work; you need activated carbon filters specifically certified for PFAS removal. Whole-house systems are more effective than point-of-use filters but also more expensive. Focus on filtering water used for drinking and cooking, where exposure matters most.

Advocate for Your Utility to Replace Lead Service Lines

Attend public utility meetings and demand faster lead service line replacement timelines. If your home is connected via a lead service line, you can often get your half of the line replaced at low or no cost through utility programs. Contact your water utility to learn about available programs.

Reduce Corrosion Risk in Your Home

Until lead service lines are replaced, minimizing corrosion risk is important. Don't boil water to try to remove lead (boiling concentrates lead). Keep water moving through your pipes by flushing regularly. If you'll be away from home for extended periods, flush all taps when you return before using water for drinking or cooking. Consider having your home's internal plumbing inspected if it's older; outdated brass fittings and solder contain lead.

Check Your Utility's Water Quality Information

Get a copy of your utility's annual water quality report. Look for which contaminants were detected, at what levels, and whether they exceeded action levels. If you see concerning information, contact your utility for clarification and ask what steps they're taking to address the issues.

Looking Forward: New Jersey's Water Future

New Jersey faces billions of dollars in water infrastructure needs. Lead service line replacement, PFAS treatment, aging pipe replacement, and improved water treatment infrastructure will require sustained investment over decades. The state has demonstrated commitment through regulation and emergency funding for crisis areas like Newark and Jersey City, but long-term solutions require ongoing political will and funding.

The good news is that New Jersey's regulatory framework is among the strongest in the nation. The state's strict PFAS standards, aggressive lead service line replacement timelines, and comprehensive testing requirements put it ahead of most states in addressing water quality. Consumer Confidence Reports provide transparency, and utilities are increasingly investing in modern treatment technologies.

However, individual vigilance remains essential. Water quality isn't guaranteed by regulation alone. Regular testing, prompt action when problems are identified, and advocacy for infrastructure investment are how New Jersey residents protect themselves. Use free tools like ClearWater to understand what contaminants have been detected in your area, review your utility's water quality reports, and test your own water. These steps, combined with reasonable filtration and awareness, can significantly reduce your family's exposure to contaminants while supporting broader efforts to improve infrastructure across the state.

title: Florida Tap Water Quality: What's in Your Tap Water?
excerpt: Florida's tap water faces unique challenges from naturally high minerals, PFAS contamination, and climate threats. Learn what's in your water and how to protect your family.
content:
If you live in Florida, you might assume your tap water is as pure as the state's pristine beaches. The reality is more complex. Florida's drinking water faces a distinctive set of challenges shaped by geology, military presence, coastal vulnerability, and climate change. Understanding what comes out of your tap matters for your health and your family's safety. This guide breaks down Florida's water quality picture, explains the risks specific to your region, and shows you how to take action.

Florida's Unique Water Challenge: The Floridan Aquifer

Most of Florida's drinking water comes from a single source: the Floridan Aquifer. This massive underground water system stretches across the entire state and into parts of Georgia, South Carolina, and Alabama. While the aquifer is generally reliable, its geology creates a specific water quality problem that Florida residents need to understand.

The Floridan Aquifer sits in ancient limestone formations rich in naturally occurring minerals. As water filters through this limestone for thousands of years, it dissolves minerals including calcium, magnesium, and more concerning, naturally occurring radioactive elements. This is not pollution in the traditional sense, but rather a geologic fact of life in Florida. It means Florida water is naturally harder and naturally more likely to contain radium and radon than water in many other states.

What Does Naturally Hard Water Mean for You?

Hard water means higher concentrations of minerals, particularly calcium and magnesium. For your household, this translates to several real-world effects: mineral buildup on fixtures, reduced soap effectiveness, potential scaling in water heaters, and higher mineral content in your drinking water. Hard water is not a health hazard according to the EPA, but it can be annoying and costly. Many Florida residents invest in water softeners or filtration systems to address this.

Naturally Occurring Radium and Radon: The Radioactive Reality

The most significant natural contamination concern in Florida is radium. Radium is a naturally radioactive element present in some Florida groundwater. The EPA sets maximum contaminant levels (MCL) for two types of radium: combined radium-226 and radium-228 combined cannot exceed 5 picocuries per liter (pCi/L). Florida water systems regularly test for radium, and systems that exceed this limit are required to install treatment to reduce levels.

Radon, another radioactive gas that occurs naturally in Florida groundwater, is less commonly regulated at the federal level for drinking water, though the EPA has proposed a maximum contaminant level of 300 pCi/L. Radon can enter homes through groundwater, particularly from private wells. If you use a private well in Florida, radon testing is highly recommended, as it poses a greater risk to lung health than to drinking water safety per se.

PFAS Contamination: The Manufactured Threat

While the Floridan Aquifer brings natural contaminants, human activity has introduced synthetic chemicals that are far more dangerous and harder to address. PFAS, or per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, are human-made chemicals used in industrial processes, firefighting foams, and non-stick coatings. They are called forever chemicals because they do not break down in the environment or in your body.

Where PFAS Enters Florida's Water

Florida's PFAS problem is heavily concentrated in areas surrounding military bases, particularly Naval Air Station Pensacola and Eglin Air Force Base in the Panhandle, where aqueous film-forming foams (AFFF) containing PFAS were used for decades. These bases are not the only sources. PFAS also comes from industrial manufacturing sites, airports, and landfills across the state. Groundwater contamination from these sources has affected municipal water supplies serving thousands of Floridians.

In Pensacola, testing has detected PFAS compounds in drinking water supplies at levels exceeding proposed EPA health advisories. While the EPA has not yet set a firm maximum contaminant level for most PFAS, the agency issued health advisory levels in 2022 that are far lower than previous guidance. For PFOA and PFOS, the most studied PFAS chemicals, the current health advisory is effectively zero, meaning any detectable level is a concern.

Health Risks of PFAS Exposure

PFAS chemicals accumulate in your body and are linked to increased cholesterol levels, liver damage, thyroid disease, immune system suppression, and developmental effects in children. Long-term exposure is the concern, as PFAS builds up in your system over years. Studies show that people who drink contaminated water for extended periods have measurable PFAS in their blood. For families in affected areas, this is not a theoretical risk but a documented one.

Disinfection Byproducts: The Cost of Clean Water

Florida water systems face a paradox: the water is naturally rich in organic compounds due to the state's wetlands and swampy terrain. When utilities add chlorine or other disinfectants to kill harmful bacteria and viruses, those chemicals react with organic matter to form disinfection byproducts (DBPs), primarily trihalomethanes (THMs) and haloacetic acids (HAAs). These byproducts are regulated, but violations occur regularly in Florida systems.

The EPA's maximum contaminant level for total trihalomethanes is 80 parts per billion (ppb), and for haloacetic acids (five specific compounds) is 60 ppb. In coastal cities like Miami, Tampa, and Jacksonville, which draw water from sources with high natural organic content, DBP violations are not uncommon. A violation means the water utility has allowed levels to exceed safe limits, requiring notification to the public and corrective action.

Why This Happens in Florida

Florida's subtropical climate and extensive wetlands and marshes mean that source water naturally contains high levels of dissolved organic matter. This material, while not inherently toxic, becomes problematic when disinfected. Utilities must balance the need to disinfect against the risk of creating byproducts. Some systems have invested in advanced treatment like ozonation, ultraviolet light, or activated carbon to reduce organic matter before disinfection. Others rely on traditional chlorination and manage DBP levels as best they can.

How Florida's Major Cities Compare

Water quality varies significantly across Florida. Major metropolitan areas have different source waters, treatment approaches, and regulatory histories. Here is what you should know about the major cities.

Miami and Miami-Dade County

Miami-Dade's water comes primarily from the Biscayne Aquifer, a shallower formation than the Floridan Aquifer. The system serves over 2 million people and has reported violations related to disinfection byproducts in recent years, particularly in coastal distribution areas. The main concerns are DBPs from treating organically rich water and hardness typical of South Florida. Saltwater intrusion is an emerging threat due to rising sea levels, as we discuss below.

Tampa and Hillsborough County

Tampa's water utility draws from multiple sources including groundwater and surface water from the Hillsborough River. The system has dealt with occasional DBP violations and naturally high mineral content. Tampa is investing in advanced treatment to address water quality challenges and is exploring alternative sources as climate change impacts grow.

Orlando and Central Florida

Orlando's water comes from the Floridan Aquifer and is generally of good quality with fewer reported violations than coastal systems. However, naturally high hardness and occasional trace amounts of radium are concerns. The region's rapid growth has put pressure on water supplies, but treatment is fairly consistent.

Jacksonville and Duval County

Jacksonville's water system is one of the state's largest and relies on Floridan Aquifer groundwater. The system has reported disinfection byproduct violations, particularly in areas with high organic content source water. The St. Johns River, which influences some of the system's water sources, adds to organic content challenges.

Fort Lauderdale and Broward County

Fort Lauderdale's utility serves a dense coastal population and faces challenges similar to Miami, including saltwater intrusion threats and DBP management. The city has invested significantly in treatment infrastructure to maintain safety standards as environmental pressures increase.

Saltwater Intrusion: Florida's Climate Change Water Crisis

Florida's biggest water quality threat may not be a chemical but rather a physical change: saltwater intrusion. As sea levels rise due to climate change, saltwater is moving inland into the freshwater aquifers that supply drinking water. This is not a future threat; it is already happening.

The Floridan Aquifer, which provides drinking water to millions of Floridians, interfaces with saltwater along the coast. As freshwater is pumped out for human use and as sea levels rise, the boundary between fresh and saltwater shifts inland. In some coastal areas, aquifers that were purely freshwater decades ago now contain measurable saltwater contamination.

Why Saltwater Intrusion Matters

Salt in drinking water is a health concern for people with hypertension or on sodium-restricted diets. More broadly, if saltwater infiltrates the primary aquifer, it contaminates vast quantities of water and is extremely difficult to treat economically. Desalination is possible but expensive. Some Florida utilities are already planning for this scenario with investment in alternative supplies and advanced treatment. Homeowners in coastal areas may face higher water costs and reduced reliability in the coming decades.

How to Find Out What's in Your Tap Water

Your water utility is required by law to provide you with a Consumer Confidence Report (CCR) annually. This report lists all contaminants detected in your drinking water and whether any exceed safe levels. You can find your utility's CCR on their website, usually under the water quality or utilities section. However, these reports are dense and technical. For a clear summary of your specific water quality, use ClearWater's free ZIP code lookup tool at checkclearwater.com. Enter your ZIP code and get a straightforward summary of what the EPA data shows about your local water system, including contaminants detected, violation history, and whether you should consider additional treatment.

What You Can Do Today

Understanding your water quality is the first step. Here are concrete actions you can take right now.

Get Your Water Quality Report

Request your utility's Consumer Confidence Report if you do not have it. If your utility is not responsive, contact your state health department. Document any violations, particularly recent ones, as these indicate active concerns.

Know Your Source

Call your water utility and ask specifically about your source water: groundwater, surface water, or a blend. Ask about any known contaminants, recent violations, and whether your area has PFAS concerns. Many utilities have water quality specialists who can provide this information.

Test Your Tap Water

For private wells in Florida, testing is essential. The University of Florida and local health departments can recommend certified laboratories. For municipal water, testing is less critical since the utility tests continuously, but if you have specific concerns, a home test can provide peace of mind. Focus on contaminants of concern for your area: PFAS in Pensacola and Panhandle counties, DBPs in coastal cities, radium statewide.

Consider Filtration

Depending on your water quality and concerns, home filtration ranges from simple pitcher filters to whole-home systems. Pitcher filters (using activated carbon) address taste, odor, and some chemicals but not everything. Whole-home systems can address hardness, some DBPs, and other contaminants. For PFAS, you need specific activated carbon systems rated for PFAS removal. Research options that match your specific contaminants of concern rather than buying generic solutions.

Stay Informed About Saltwater Intrusion

If you live in a coastal area, monitor news about saltwater intrusion in your region. Some utilities have started designing water supplies with alternative sources and advanced treatment to prepare. Ask your utility about their long-term plan for climate resilience. Your voice matters in utility planning.

The Bottom Line

Florida's tap water is safe by federal standards in most places most of the time. However, the state's unique geology, PFAS contamination in specific regions, disinfection byproduct challenges in many utilities, and emerging climate threats mean that Floridians face water quality considerations more complex than many Americans. Your water is regularly tested and regulated, but knowing what is in it and whether additional steps make sense for your household is wise. Start with a free lookup of your local water quality data, read your utility's quality report, and then decide whether additional measures suit your situation and your family's needs.

title: Texas Tap Water Quality: What You Need to Know in 2026
excerpt: Texas tap water quality varies widely by region. Learn about TTHM violations, aging infrastructure, and how to check your local water system's EPA record.
content:
Texas is a vast state with equally diverse water systems, from the sprawling metropolis of Houston to rural communities relying on well water across West Texas. While many Texas residents enjoy safe, clean tap water, others face ongoing challenges with contaminants, aging infrastructure, and treatment limitations. Understanding what's in your tap water and where potential risks exist is essential for protecting your family's health. This guide breaks down Texas water quality by region, explains the science behind common contaminants, and shows you exactly how to look up your own water system's EPA violations and test results.

Texas Water Quality Overview: A State of Contrasts

Texas water systems serve more than 30 million people through hundreds of public water utilities, each with different challenges. Some systems, like San Antonio's Edwards Aquifer, maintain excellent water quality naturally. Others, like Houston and El Paso, have battled persistent violations with disinfection byproducts for decades. Rural well water in West Texas faces naturally occurring contamination that surface water systems don't typically encounter.

The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) sets legal limits called Maximum Contaminant Levels, or MCLs, for over 90 different drinking water contaminants. Public water systems must test regularly and report results to the EPA. However, not all contaminants are regulated, and not all regulated contaminants are detected and reported by every system. This variation, combined with aging infrastructure and weather-related disruptions, makes Texas a complex landscape for water quality.

Houston's Tap Water: Surface Water, TTHM Violations, and Treatment Challenges

Why Houston Struggles with Disinfection Byproducts

Houston's Metropolitan Transit Authority (HMTUA) and the Houston Water Works serve over 2 million people using surface water from reservoirs, lakes, and rivers. Surface water requires much more aggressive disinfection than groundwater because it's exposed to the environment and carries higher levels of organic matter.

When water treatment plants add chlorine to kill bacteria and viruses, the chlorine reacts with natural organic matter in the water, creating byproducts called trihalomethanes (THMs). The EPA's Maximum Contaminant Level for total THMs is 80 parts per billion (ppb). For decades, Houston has struggled to stay under this limit, particularly during warm months when water temperature and organic matter levels rise.

In recent years, Houston has experienced violations of the TTHM standard, especially in areas farther from the treatment plants. The farther water travels through pipes, the longer it sits in the system, and the more THMs can form. This is why some Houston neighborhoods report higher TTHM levels than others, even though they drink from the same treatment plants.

What TTHM Means for Houston Families

THMs are regulated because long-term exposure may increase cancer risk, though the exact threshold for human health effects remains debated among scientists. The EPA's 80 ppb standard is considered safe for lifetime consumption, even by children and pregnant women. However, if your water system has reported TTHM violations, it means the system exceeded this standard during testing periods.

Houston water officials have invested hundreds of millions in new treatment technologies, including activated carbon filters and ozone treatment, which are more effective at removing organic matter before chlorination. These efforts have reduced violations in many areas, but challenges persist.

What You Can Do in Houston

  • Check your water system's latest EPA violation reports using ClearWater's free lookup tool by entering your ZIP code. This will show you if TTHM violations have been reported in your area.
  • If violations are present, consider using a water filter certified for disinfection byproduct reduction, such as a pitcher filter or under-sink system with activated carbon, which removes some THMs.
  • Run tap water for 30 seconds before using for drinking or cooking if you haven't used a faucet in several hours, as this helps clear standing water with higher contaminant concentrations.

San Antonio's Edwards Aquifer: A Model of Natural Water Quality

Why San Antonio's Water Is Consistently Clean

San Antonio Water System (SAWS) serves approximately 1.6 million people primarily from the Edwards Aquifer, a massive underground limestone formation that naturally filters water. Groundwater that percolates through limestone is naturally filtered through hundreds of feet of rock, removing many contaminants and naturally alkalizing the water.

Because the Edwards Aquifer's water undergoes this natural filtration and comes from a protected underground source, San Antonio typically reports fewer contaminants and fewer EPA violations than surface water systems like Houston. The water naturally contains beneficial minerals like calcium and magnesium, which is why San Antonio water is somewhat hard, but not dangerously so.

San Antonio's Water Quality Record

Over the past decade, SAWS has maintained an excellent safety record with minimal reportable violations. The system does treat water with chlorine for disinfection and adjusts pH to prevent pipe corrosion, but the starting water quality is so high that final treated water typically meets or exceeds EPA standards with comfortable margins.

This does not mean San Antonio residents should ignore water quality. The Edwards Aquifer is increasingly strained by drought and population growth, and all groundwater can be vulnerable to contamination if surface pollutants infiltrate through cracks or poorly sealed wells. However, residents can generally feel confident in their tap water's safety without extensive additional treatment.

Dallas-Fort Worth: Aging Water Infrastructure and Pipeline Corrosion

The Problem with Old Pipes

The Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex, serving approximately 7 million people, operates water systems that combine surface water from lakes and reservoirs with groundwater. Much of the distribution infrastructure is 50 to 100 years old. Older pipes, particularly those made of galvanized steel or cast iron, corrode over time, potentially leaching lead and copper into drinking water.

The EPA regulates lead at an Action Level of 15 parts per billion (ppb), meaning water systems must take corrosion control measures if more than 10 percent of samples exceed this level. Lead is particularly harmful to children, interfering with brain development and learning. Copper, regulated at 1.3 ppm (parts per million), can cause gastrointestinal distress at high levels.

Dallas-Fort Worth's Lead and Corrosion Control Efforts

Major Dallas and Fort Worth water utilities, including Dallas Water Utilities and Fort Worth Water Department, have implemented lead service line replacement programs and corrosion control treatment, adding alkalinity adjustments to reduce pipe corrosion. However, the scale of infrastructure replacement is enormous, and progress is gradual.

The 2021 winter storm caused significant strain on these aging systems, forcing boil-water advisories across the region and temporarily reducing water quality monitoring. Some systems reported lead exceedances in the months following the storm as water pressure changes destabilized corroded pipes.

Protecting Your Family in DFW

  • Request a lead and copper test report from your local water utility. If you rent, contact your landlord. If you own, the utility can provide information on how to test your home's water.
  • If your home was built before 1986, assume your service line may contain lead. Run water for 1 to 2 minutes before using for drinking or cooking, especially first thing in the morning, to flush out standing water that may have absorbed lead from pipes.
  • Use cold water for drinking, cooking, and baby formula. Hot water corrodes pipes faster and may contain higher lead levels.
  • Consider having your water tested through a certified lab, particularly if you have young children or are pregnant.

El Paso's Persistent TTHM Challenge

Why El Paso's Water System Struggles

El Paso Water Utilities serves approximately 680,000 people using a combination of surface water from the Rio Grande and groundwater from the Hueco Bolson Aquifer. Like Houston, El Paso has battled TTHM violations for years, sometimes exceeding the EPA's 80 ppb limit.

El Paso's challenges are compounded by climate factors. The city receives less than 10 inches of rain annually, making surface water supply unreliable and leading to more reliance on groundwater. Additionally, the Rio Grande contains naturally high levels of organic matter, particularly during rainy seasons upstream in New Mexico, making disinfection more difficult and creating more byproducts.

Treatment Improvements and Ongoing Work

El Paso Water has invested in advanced treatment technologies, including membrane filtration and ozone treatment, to reduce organic matter before chlorination. These efforts have reduced TTHM levels significantly in recent years. However, the system remains vulnerable to seasonal spikes and infrastructure limitations.

The 2021 winter storm also affected El Paso's water system, though less severely than Dallas-Fort Worth. However, it highlighted the infrastructure's vulnerability to extreme weather.

West Texas Well Water: Natural Contamination and Private System Challenges

Nitrates and Arsenic in Rural West Texas

Rural communities and ranches across West Texas often rely on private wells rather than municipal water systems. Unlike public systems, private wells are not regulated by the EPA and are the homeowner's responsibility to test and maintain.

West Texas groundwater naturally contains elevated levels of nitrates and arsenic in many areas. Nitrates, typically from agricultural runoff or septic systems, are regulated at 10 ppm by the EPA. High nitrate levels can cause a dangerous condition called methemoglobinemia, or blue baby syndrome, in infants under six months old.

Arsenic, a naturally occurring element in some geological formations, is regulated at 10 parts per billion (ppb). Chronic arsenic exposure increases cancer risk and can cause skin problems and organ damage. West Texas aquifers, particularly in areas with certain mineral deposits, naturally contain arsenic above the EPA's safe level.

Testing and Treatment Options for Private Wells

If you rely on well water in West Texas, you should test your water at least once yearly for nitrates and arsenic, and ideally every few years. County health departments or certified private labs can conduct these tests for $50 to $300, depending on the number of contaminants tested.

If nitrates or arsenic are found above EPA limits, treatment options include reverse osmosis filters (which remove both), distillation, or ion exchange systems. These can be point-of-use systems (for a single faucet) or whole-house systems, depending on your needs and budget.

The 2021 Winter Storm's Impact on Texas Water Infrastructure

What Happened During the Freeze

In February 2021, an unusually severe winter storm hit Texas, causing temperatures to plummet below freezing for days. Water pipes froze and burst across the state, forcing utilities to issue boil-water notices and leading to water quality monitoring challenges. Some systems lost water pressure entirely, and the disruption lasted weeks in some areas.

Long-Term Water Quality Implications

The winter storm revealed just how vulnerable Texas water infrastructure is to weather extremes. In many cases, the disruption damaged water quality monitoring equipment, prevented proper testing, and led to bacterial contamination as pressure dropped. Some systems reported elevated levels of total coliform bacteria, a warning sign of contamination risk.

In the years following the storm, Texas water utilities have invested more heavily in infrastructure hardening and emergency preparedness. However, aging pipes and aging systems remain vulnerable. If you experienced water quality problems after the 2021 storm, continue monitoring your system's reported violations on the EPA's website or through ClearWater.

How to Look Up Your Texas Water System's EPA Record

What Information Is Publicly Available

Every public water system in Texas must provide a Consumer Confidence Report (CCR) to all customers annually. These reports detail which contaminants were tested for, what was found, whether any EPA violations occurred, and what treatment steps are being taken. These reports are available online from your water utility's website.

Additionally, the EPA maintains a database of all reported violations and test results. You can access this information through the EPA's Safe Drinking Water Information System (SDWIS), though the interface is technical and not user-friendly for general consumers.

Using ClearWater for Quick Answers

The easiest way to check your Texas water system's EPA violations and water quality history is to use a free lookup tool like ClearWater. Simply enter your ZIP code on ClearWater's website, and the tool will pull your water system's reported violations, contaminants detected, and enforcement actions taken by the EPA. This takes the guesswork out of navigating government databases and gives you a clear summary in plain language.

Step-by-Step: What to Look For in Your Water Report

  1. Identify your water utility. Check your water bill or contact your local city or county.
  2. Search ClearWater's database by ZIP code or utility name.
  3. Review the violation history. Look for patterns. A single old violation may have been resolved; repeated recent violations suggest ongoing problems.
  4. Check which contaminants were detected and at what levels. Compare to EPA MCLs to understand the significance.
  5. Request your utility's most recent Consumer Confidence Report for detailed treatment information.
  6. If violations are present and concern you, contact your water utility's public information office with questions. They're required to answer.

Contaminants Commonly Found in Texas Water Systems

Disinfection Byproducts (THMs and HAAs)

As discussed, THMs (trihalomethanes) form when chlorine reacts with organic matter. A related group called haloacetic acids (HAAs) form the same way and are regulated at 60 ppb. Both are found most often in surface water systems like Houston, El Paso, and some Dallas-Fort Worth systems.

Lead and Copper

Leached from corroded pipes, lead and copper are most common in older systems with aging infrastructure. Dallas-Fort Worth and parts of Houston are at higher risk than systems with newer pipes.

Bacteria and Viruses

While rare in properly treated systems, total coliform bacteria can appear after water main breaks or pressure loss. The 2021 winter storm caused several temporary bacterial detections across Texas. Viruses are similarly rare but can indicate treatment failure.

Naturally Occurring Contaminants

Arsenic, uranium, and radon occur naturally in some Texas groundwater and are regulated by EPA. Radium, another naturally occurring radioactive element, is also found in some systems and is regulated at 5 pCi/L (picocuries per liter).

Steps to Take Today to Protect Your Texas Water

Understanding your local water quality is important, but knowledge without action is incomplete. Here's what you can do immediately.

  • Look up your water system's EPA record using ClearWater or your utility's website. Take 10 minutes today to understand what's in your tap water.
  • If you have young children, are pregnant, or have a compromised immune system, consider having your tap water tested by a certified lab, particularly for lead, bacteria, and nitrates if you're on a private well.
  • Inspect your home's pipes if it was built before 1986. Look for blue or green corrosion stains near copper fittings or gray corrosion on galvanized steel. These suggest corrosion is occurring.
  • If your home has a lead service line or you're unsure, start using cold water only for drinking and cooking, and run water for 1 to 2 minutes before use.
  • If you use a private well in West Texas, schedule a test for nitrates and arsenic immediately if you haven't tested in the past year.
  • Stay informed. Bookmark your water utility's website and check for new violation reports or boil-water notices during extreme weather.

Texas Water Quality: The Bottom Line

Texas is a large and varied state, and tap water quality depends entirely on where you live. Some regions, like San Antonio, enjoy naturally excellent water quality with minimal violations. Others, like Houston and El Paso, battle persistent challenges with disinfection byproducts. Infrastructure age and regional geology both play significant roles in determining what's in your tap water.

The good news is that information is available and free. Federal law requires water systems to test regularly and report results. By understanding your local water system's challenges and checking for violations, you can make informed decisions about whether additional treatment is right for your family. Texas residents are not powerless in the face of water quality challenges. Take the first step today by looking up your water system's EPA record, and then decide what additional protection makes sense for your home.

title: California Tap Water Quality: What's in the Water Across the State?
excerpt: California's tap water faces unique challenges from nitrates, arsenic, and PFAS. Learn what's in your water and how to protect your family.
content:
California supplies drinking water to nearly 40 million people across one of the world's most agriculturally productive regions. Yet the Golden State's water quality picture is far from golden for many communities. While major cities like Los Angeles and San Francisco generally maintain safe water supplies that meet federal standards, vast areas of rural California face serious contamination challenges that often go unnoticed by residents who assume their tap water is safe.

From the nitrate-saturated groundwater of the Central Valley to the arsenic-tainted wells of the Mojave Desert, California's water quality varies dramatically depending on where you live. The state also deals with emerging contaminants like PFAS near military bases and industrial sites. Understanding what's actually in your tap water isn't just a matter of curiosity, it's a matter of public health, especially for families with young children, pregnant women, and people with compromised immune systems.

This article breaks down California's most pressing water quality issues, explains why they exist, and shows you concrete steps to protect your family's health.

The California Water Quality Landscape: Why It Matters

California's water challenges stem from a unique combination of geography, agriculture, climate, and demographics. The state relies on a complex patchwork of water sources: surface water from mountain snowmelt and reservoirs, groundwater from aquifers, and imported water from neighboring states. This diversity means that water quality varies not just between regions, but sometimes between neighborhoods.

California also operates under some of the nation's strictest water quality regulations. The state has more drinking water contaminant standards than federal law requires. For example, California's drinking water standard for hexavalent chromium (a known carcinogen) is 10 parts per billion (ppb), while the federal government has not set a maximum contaminant level (MCL) at all. This means California water systems must meet higher thresholds than most states, which sounds protective until you realize that many small water systems, especially those serving rural and farmworker communities, struggle to comply.

To understand what's specifically in your tap water, you can use ClearWater's free EPA drinking water lookup tool by entering your ZIP code. It pulls official water quality data from your local system's annual Consumer Confidence Report.

Nitrate Contamination in the Central Valley: A Crisis in Agricultural Country

What's Happening and Why

The San Joaquin Valley and surrounding agricultural regions of California face a silent public health crisis: widespread nitrate contamination in groundwater. Nitrates are nitrogen compounds that leach into groundwater from agricultural fertilizers, animal waste, and septic systems. California's federal MCL for nitrate is 10 milligrams per liter (mg/L), the same as the EPA standard, but many small water systems in farm regions consistently exceed this level.

The reason is straightforward. Decades of intensive agriculture in California's Central Valley, combined with shallow groundwater aquifers and permeable soil, created perfect conditions for nitrate contamination. A 2018 University of California study found that nitrate concentrations above the safe drinking water standard affected approximately 250,000 people living in rural areas of the San Joaquin Valley, many of them farmworker communities with limited access to alternative water sources.

Who Is Most Affected

Small water systems serving fewer than 3,300 people bear the heaviest burden. These include:

  • Private domestic well systems in rural areas
  • Community water systems serving farmworker housing
  • Small municipal systems in agricultural counties like Kern, Tulare, and Kings

Large urban systems like the Los Angeles Department of Water and Power and San Francisco's water system have the resources and regulatory oversight to blend contaminated groundwater with cleaner sources or treat water to remove nitrates. Small systems often do not.

Health Effects of Nitrate Exposure

Elevated nitrate exposure poses particular risks to infants under six months old, who can develop a condition called methemoglobinemia, or "blue baby syndrome." In this rare but serious condition, nitrates interfere with the blood's ability to carry oxygen. Pregnant women and people with certain gastrointestinal disorders also face increased vulnerability. The EPA set the MCL of 10 mg/L specifically to protect infants.

Some research also suggests that long-term nitrate exposure may increase cancer risk, though this link remains under investigation.

Arsenic in Small Groundwater Systems: The Mojave and Beyond

Natural Occurrence and Geographic Pattern

Arsenic contamination in California's drinking water is primarily a natural problem, not industrial. Arsenic naturally occurs in bedrock and soil throughout the southwestern United States. When groundwater flows through arsenic-bearing minerals, it can pick up dangerous concentrations of the element. The Mojave Desert region, parts of the Sierra Nevada foothills, and some Central Valley communities experience the highest naturally occurring arsenic levels.

California's drinking water standard for arsenic is 10 ppb, matching the federal MCL. This standard took effect in 2006 after the EPA lowered it from 50 ppb based on mounting evidence linking arsenic to skin cancer, lung cancer, bladder cancer, and kidney cancer at lower exposure levels than previously thought.

Who Is Affected

Again, small water systems and private wells are most vulnerable. Some groundwater systems in the Mojave, Inyo County, and portions of Kern County regularly report arsenic levels above the standard. Many small systems lack the financial resources or technical expertise to install effective arsenic removal systems, which typically use ion exchange or reverse osmosis technology.

Homeowners with private wells in these regions face particular risk, as private wells are not regulated by the EPA and the state does not routinely test them. If you rely on a private well in a known arsenic-risk area, testing is your responsibility.

PFAS Contamination: An Emerging Threat Near Military and Industrial Sites

What Are PFAS and Where Do They Come From

PFAS stands for per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, a large family of human-made chemicals used since the 1950s to make products water and grease resistant. They appear in firefighting foam (aqueous film-forming foams or AFFF), non-stick cookware, stain repellents, food packaging, and many industrial applications. PFAS are sometimes called "forever chemicals" because they don't break down in the environment or human body.

PFAS contamination of drinking water is primarily associated with:

  • Military bases and airports where AFFF was used for firefighting training and emergency response
  • Industrial manufacturing facilities
  • Landfills and waste disposal sites
  • Aqueous film-forming foam used by some civilian fire departments

California's PFAS Response and Affected Areas

California has taken a leadership role on PFAS, establishing drinking water standards before the federal government finalized national regulations. The state set an MCL of 13 parts per trillion (ppt) for PFOA and 14 ppt for PFOS (two specific PFAS chemicals) in 2018 and 2019 respectively, some of the lowest in the nation.

Contamination hotspots include areas surrounding Camp Pendleton (San Diego County), Edwards Air Force Base (Kern County), and several other military installations. Some civilian areas near airports and industrial sites have also detected PFAS above California's standards.

Health Concerns

Scientific evidence links PFAS exposure to elevated cholesterol, thyroid disease, liver damage, weakened immune function, kidney cancer, and testicular cancer. The EPA classified PFOA and PFOS as "likely carcinogenic to humans" in 2022. While California's strict standards provide more protection than federal minimums, any exposure carries some risk.

If you live near a military base or airport, or in an area where PFAS has been detected, check your local water quality report using ClearWater to see if PFAS appears in your system's latest data.

California's Strict Regulatory Environment: A Double-Edged Sword

How California Leads on Water Standards

California doesn't just follow federal drinking water standards, it often exceeds them. The state has established MCLs for at least 60 contaminants, compared to about 90 at the federal level, and in many cases California's limits are stricter. Examples include:

  • Hexavalent chromium: California standard 10 ppb, federal standard none (not regulated)
  • Nitrate: Same at 10 mg/L federally and in California, but California enforces more aggressively
  • PFAS chemicals: California set limits years before federal standards emerged

This reflects California's precautionary principle: when evidence suggests a chemical poses health risks, the state acts even before complete scientific consensus exists.

The Compliance Challenge

Here's the problem: stricter standards are only protective if water systems can actually comply. Small systems serving poor communities often lack the funding to invest in treatment technology or infrastructure upgrades. When a small rural water system discovers it cannot meet California's hexavalent chromium standard, for example, it faces enormous expense to install chromium-removal systems while serving communities with limited ability to pay higher water bills.

This creates a perverse situation where California's protective regulations sometimes strain the small systems that serve the most vulnerable populations most heavily.

Drought, Climate Change, and Water Quality

How Drought Affects Contaminant Concentration

During California's severe multi-year droughts, water quality challenges intensify. When surface water becomes scarce, water systems rely more heavily on groundwater, which may contain higher contaminant concentrations. Additionally, lower water availability means treatment plants cannot blend contaminated sources with clean water as effectively.

Drought also stresses the agricultural economy, sometimes leading to increased fertilizer application on drier land, which paradoxically increases the risk of nitrate leaching when irrigation does occur. Climate change is likely to intensify these patterns, with California experiencing longer droughts punctuated by intense precipitation events.

Diversification and Quality Trade-offs

As traditional water sources become less reliable, California has turned to water recycling, desalination, and groundwater storage. These sources are generally safe when properly treated, but they introduce new management challenges and occasionally new contaminants. Recycled water systems, for instance, must be carefully maintained to prevent any mixing with potable supplies.

Water Quality in California's Major Cities

Los Angeles Metropolitan Area

The Los Angeles Department of Water and Power serves about 3.9 million people. The system blends groundwater, surface water from the Eastern Sierra, and increasingly, recycled water. Major contaminants historically included perchlorate (a rocket fuel component used in the aerospace industry) and some PFAS. However, LA's large size and financial resources enable sophisticated treatment. Recent water quality reports show the system meets all federal and state standards for the vast majority of residents, though some neighborhoods have occasionally seen elevated disinfection byproducts.

San Francisco Bay Area

The San Francisco Public Utilities Commission serves 2.7 million people across a four-county region with primarily surface water from the Sierra Nevada. Water quality is generally excellent, with few contamination issues. The system's main challenge is maintaining aging infrastructure rather than contaminant problems.

San Diego

San Diego County Water Authority serves 3.3 million people through a mix of imported surface water (largely from the Colorado River and Northern California) and local groundwater. PFAS contamination near Camp Pendleton has been a concern, but aggressive treatment and monitoring have kept drinking water standards met for most residents. San Diego also leads California in water recycling, with advanced treatment ensuring recycled water quality.

Sacramento

Sacramento's water system relies on surface water from the Sacramento and American Rivers. Historically, the system has faced fewer contamination issues than groundwater-dependent systems, though aging pipes and occasional agricultural runoff create periodic challenges. Sacramento serves about 500,000 people directly through the city utility.

The Key Difference

Large urban systems generally perform better than small rural systems because they have technical expertise, financial resources for treatment, regulatory oversight, and the ability to blend water from multiple sources. If you live in a major California city, your water is likely to be safer than if you live in a small rural community, all else being equal.

What You Can Do Right Now: Actionable Steps

Step 1: Know Your Water Source and Quality

Every public water system in America must produce an annual Consumer Confidence Report (also called a Water Quality Report) and provide it free to customers. You can request this report from your water utility directly, or use ClearWater's free lookup tool by entering your ZIP code to see official water quality data including contaminants detected, levels found, and health effects. This is the most reliable starting point.

If you have a private well, contact your county health department for a list of certified water testing labs. Well water should be tested at least annually and always after flooding or significant weather events.

Step 2: Assess Your Personal Risk

Consider whether you or anyone in your household falls into a vulnerable population:

  • Infants under six months (at higher risk from nitrates)
  • Pregnant women (at higher risk from nitrates and some other contaminants)
  • People with compromised immune systems or gastrointestinal disease
  • Elderly individuals

If you're in a vulnerable group and live in an area with detected contamination, water treatment becomes more important.

Step 3: Choose Appropriate Water Treatment

Not all contaminants require the same treatment approach:

  • For nitrates: reverse osmosis, ion exchange, or specialized treatment systems
  • For arsenic: reverse osmosis, distillation, or ion exchange
  • For PFAS: specialized activated carbon or reverse osmosis
  • For general peace of mind: pitcher filters with activated carbon (help with taste and chlorine, limited effectiveness on other contaminants)

Pitcher filters and faucet-mounted filters are inexpensive and convenient but provide limited protection. Whole-house systems or dedicated treatment systems offer more comprehensive protection but require higher investment and maintenance.

Step 4: Advocate at Local and State Levels

Attend your water utility's board meetings. Comment on water quality reports. Support local efforts to upgrade infrastructure in small systems. California's water challenges won't be solved by individual action alone, they require systemic investment and political will.

Step 5: Stay Informed About Changes

Water quality isn't static. Systems discover new contamination, treatment processes change, and new regulations take effect. Subscribe to your water utility's email updates and check their website annually for new water quality reports.

The Bottom Line

California's water quality landscape is complex and geographically varied. While large urban areas generally maintain safe drinking water that meets rigorous state and federal standards, rural communities, especially those dependent on groundwater and small water systems, face persistent challenges from nitrate, arsenic, and emerging contaminants like PFAS.

The state's leadership on water quality standards is admirable but creates compliance challenges for small systems serving some of California's most vulnerable populations. Drought and climate change add urgency to these issues.

As a California resident, your first step is knowing what's actually in your tap water. Check your local water quality report. If contaminants concern you, assess whether treatment is appropriate for your situation. And remember that safe water is not a luxury good, it's a fundamental public health necessity that deserves attention and investment, especially in communities that have been historically underserved.

title: Michigan Tap Water Quality: Flint, Detroit, PFAS, and What's Changed
excerpt: Michigan's tap water crisis explained: Flint's lead contamination, Detroit's ongoing remediation, PFAS hotspots, and what's changed since 2014.
content:
Michigan's drinking water story is one of the most consequential public health sagas of the past decade. The Flint lead crisis put Michigan on the national map for all the wrong reasons, exposing systemic failures in water treatment, government oversight, and emergency response. But Michigan's water challenges extend far beyond Flint. Today, the state grapples with legacy lead infrastructure in older cities, emerging PFAS contamination near military bases, and the long road to rebuilding public trust. Understanding what happened, why it happened, and what's changed is essential for anyone living in or considering a move to Michigan.

The Flint Water Crisis: What Went Wrong

In April 2014, the City of Flint made a cost-cutting decision that would affect hundreds of thousands of people. Facing severe financial pressure, Flint's emergency manager switched the city's water source from Detroit's Lake Huron system to the Flint River, expecting to save roughly $12 million annually. On paper, it seemed reasonable. In reality, it triggered one of America's worst public health disasters in modern times.

The Flint River's water is significantly more corrosive than Lake Huron water. Corrosive water has a higher potential to leach lead and other metals from pipes, solder, and brass fittings inside the water distribution system and in homes. When water is more acidic or has certain chemical properties, it acts like a solvent, slowly dissolving the protective mineral scale that builds up inside old pipes over decades.

Flint's water treatment plant was not equipped to properly treat this corrosive river water. The plant lacked adequate corrosion control chemicals, specifically orthophosphate, which creates a protective barrier inside pipes. Despite warnings from water quality experts and engineers, the decision to use untreated river water proceeded without the necessary treatment adjustments.

The Lead Problem in Flint's Pipes

Flint's water infrastructure is old. Many of the city's service lines (pipes running from the main water line to homes) were installed in the early 1900s and contain lead. Lead solder was also commonly used to seal pipe joints until it was banned in 1986. When corrosive Flint River water flowed through these pipes without proper corrosion control, lead leached into tap water at dangerous levels.

The EPA's current lead action level is 15 parts per billion (ppb). In Flint, water samples showed lead levels exceeding 100 ppb, and some homes tested at over 900 ppb. For perspective, the EPA classifies water with more than 15 ppb as requiring immediate action. Children exposed to lead face risks including developmental delays, learning disabilities, behavioral problems, and lower IQ scores. In adults, lead exposure increases blood pressure and kidney damage risk.

What made the crisis worse was the lack of transparency. State and local officials were slow to acknowledge the lead problem, and residents were not adequately warned to use bottled water or take precautions. Some officials claimed the water was safe when testing showed otherwise. This erosion of trust lasted years.

Systemic Failures and Accountability

The Flint crisis was not a simple technical failure. It was a cascade of decisions, omissions, and institutional failures across multiple levels of government and agencies. The Michigan Department of Environmental Quality (now Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy, or EGLE) did not properly oversee Flint's water system. The EPA was slower than it should have been to intervene. City leadership mismanaged the public response.

By late 2015, more than a year into the crisis, Flint finally began adding orthophosphate to the water supply. However, the damage to public health and community trust was already substantial. Children in Flint experienced measurable increases in lead exposure. The long-term health effects of the crisis continue to unfold.

Where Flint Stands Today

Flint switched back to Detroit's water system in October 2015, but the crisis did not end overnight. The city has spent the subsequent years addressing lead service lines. Michigan and the City of Flint have accelerated lead pipe replacement programs. As of 2023, Flint has replaced thousands of lead service lines, but the work continues. The city estimates it will take until 2030 or beyond to replace all lead service lines.
